facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a randomized controlled trial of 20 mg ethinyl estradiol oral contraceptive showed no meaningful improvement versus placebo in perimenopausal women" Section: Night Sweats.

facts.claim: Progesterone increases core temperature and requires 300 kcal/day to do so in weight-stable premenopausal women, suggesting metabolic benefit against weight gain.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "progesterone increases core temperature and requires 300 kcal/d to do that in weight stable premenopausal women" Section: Weight Gain.

facts.claim: During perimenopause, estradiol levels average 26% higher than premenopausal levels and fluctuate erratically, while ovulation and progesterone become insufficient or absent.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "E2 levels average 26% higher and soar erratically – some women describe feeling pregnant!" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: A physiological luteal phase dose of oral micronized progesterone is 300 mg at bedtime, which keeps blood progesterone above luteal phase threshold for 24 hours.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "A physiological luteal phase dose of OMP is 300 mg at bedtime – this keeps the P 4 blood level above a luteal phase threshold for 24 hours" Section: OMP Treatment.

facts.claim: Cyclic OMP for symptomatic perimenopausal women with regular cycles is given on cycle days 14-27 (14 on, 14 off) at 300 mg bedtime.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Given cyclically (cycle d 14-27, or 14 on/off) in menstruating midlife women, OMP4 decreases cyclic VMS, improves sleep and pre-menstrual mastalgia." Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: For perimenopausal menorrhagia, treatment is ibuprofen 200 mg every 6 hours plus oral micronized progesterone on cycle days 4-28.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Menorrhagia is treated with ibuprofen 200mg/6h plus OMP4 cycle d 4-28." Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: NSAIDs such as ibuprofen restore endometrial prostaglandin balance and produce a 25-50% decrease in menstrual flow.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "NSAIDs restore a normal balance of endometrial prostaglandins and thus cause a 25-50% decrease in flow" Section: Progesterone for heavy or prolonged flow.

facts.claim: Meta-analysis showed perimenopausal women had follicular phase E2 levels of 225 pmol/L (n=415) versus 175 pmol/L in premenopausal women (n=276), F=16.12, P=0.041.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "in perimenopausal women (n = 415) in the follicular phase E 2 levels were 225 pmol/L versus 175 pmol/L in premenopausal women" Section: Current Evidence for Hormonal Changes.

facts.claim: In the luteal phase, perimenopausal women (n=69) had mean E2 of 374 pmol/L compared to 300 pmol/L in premenopausal women (n=290), F=15.46, P=0.016.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "in perimenopausal women (n = 69) mean E2 levels were 374 pmol/L compared with 300 pmol/L in" Section: Current Evidence for Hormonal Changes.

facts.claim: Progesterone secretion rises 1400% above follicular phase levels, while midcycle estradiol peaks rise only 220% above its low during flow.

source.title: Progesterone for Symptomatic Perimenopause Treatment - Progesterone politics,  physiology and potential for perimenopause
source.authors: Prior JC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "rising 1400 percent above its low follicular phase levels; midcycle maximum E2 levels, by contrast, rise only 220 percent above its low during flow" Section: Physiological Rationale.

facts.claim: Phase III double-blind RCT randomized 189 perimenopausal women (93 progesterone, 96 placebo) to 300 mg oral micronized progesterone at bedtime versus placebo for 3 months following a 1-month untreated baseline.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "double-blind, randomized trial of 300 mg oral micronized progesterone@bedtime versus placebo for 3-months (m) after a 1-m untreated baseline" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: More women on progesterone than placebo reported menstrual flow had stopped during the 3-month trial (28% vs 14%, P=0.024), though overall perceived flow change did not differ.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "More women on progesterone than placebo reported that their flow had stopped (28 vs. 14 percent, P = 0.024)" Section: Sleep, anxiety, menstrual flow.

facts.claim: In a prior 3-month RCT in 132 postmenopausal women, oral micronized progesterone was superior to placebo for VMS without serious adverse events or negative cardiovascular biomarker effects.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Progesterone was superior to placebo in postmenopausal community-dwelling women (n = 132) in a 3-month VMS RCT, without serious adverse events" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: Abrupt discontinuation of progesterone produced no rapid VMS rebound over 1 month in postmenopausal women, unlike estrogen-based MHT where ~25% of women experience clinically problematic rebound VMS.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Abrupt discontinuation of progesterone produced no rapid increase in VMS over 1-month in postmenopausal women" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: Per Protocol sensitivity analysis (n=176 with >=7 days of Calendar data) showed similar non-significant 3rd-month VMS Score difference: RR 0.78 (95% CI 0.53-1.13), RD -1.58 (95% CI -4.00 to 0.84), P=0.191.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Per Protocol sensitivity analysis in 176 women were similar: RR 0.78 [95% CI 0.53, 1.13], RD - 1.58 [95% CI - 4.00, 0.84], P = 0.191" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Progesterone caused greater perceived decrease in night sweat frequency (P=0.015), night sweat intensity (P<0.001), and daytime VMS intensity (P=0.014) versus placebo.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "greater perceived decrease in night sweat frequency (P = 0.015) and decreases in both night sweat (P < 0.001) and daytime VMS intensity (P < 0.014)" Section: Women's perceived VMS changes.

facts.claim: The 3rd-month VMS Score did not differ significantly between progesterone and placebo (rate difference -1.51, 95% CI -3.97 to 0.95, P=0.222), but the CI did not exclude a 3-point minimal clinically important benefit.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Third-m VMS Score did not differ by therapy (Rate Difference - 1.51). However, the 95% CI [- 3.97, 0.95] P = 0.222" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: Women on progesterone perceived significantly decreased night sweats compared to placebo (P=0.023) and improved sleep quality (P=0.005).

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Women perceived progesterone caused decreased night sweats (P = 0.023) and improved sleep quality (P = 0.005)" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: Progesterone significantly decreased perimenopause-related life interference compared to placebo (mean difference -6.65, 95% CI -12.11 to -1.18, P=0.017) without increasing depression scores.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "it decreased perimenopause-related life interference (P = 0.017) without increased depression" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: Randomization required average 4 moderate-to-severe VMS per 24-h day for at least 2 of 4 weeks, OR at least 56 VMS per 4 weeks, OR night sweat awakenings twice weekly or more.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "average of 4 moderate-to-severe VMS per 24-h day for at least two out of four weeks...or at least 56 VMS per four-weeks" Section: Participants.

facts.claim: Baseline mean VMS Score across all 189 participants was 12.2 (SD 11.3), with average frequency of 4.9 (SD 3.8) VMS per 24-h day and average intensity of 2.3 (SD 0.7) on a 0-4 scale.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "mean (SD) baseline VMS Score for all participants was 12.2 (11.3). At baseline, the overall average frequency of VMS/24-h day was 4.9 (3.8)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Trial retention was 93% (176/189 completed), with medication adherence of 91.4% on progesterone and 84.4% on placebo by capsule counts and Calendar records.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Medication adherence was excellent by capsule counts and Calendar records (91.4% on progesterone and 84.4% on placebo)" Section: Safety.

facts.claim: No serious adverse events occurred during the 4-month trial; one progesterone-treated participant discontinued for physician-mandated new-onset atrial fibrillation, and four placebo-treated discontinued for ineffectiveness.

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "No serious adverse events occurred during this trial" Section: Safety, adverse events and adherence.

facts.claim: Two-thirds (n=126, 66.7%) of randomized participants were in Late Perimenopause (>=1 cycle >60 days) and one-third (n=63, 33.3%) in Early Perimenopause (never skipped a period).

source.title: Oral micronized progesterone for perimenopausal night sweats and hot flushes a  Phase III Canada-wide randomized placebo-controlled 4 month trial
source.authors: Prior JC, Cameron A, Fung M, Hitchcock CL, Janssen P, Lee T, Singer J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "two-thirds were in Late Perimenopause [LP (n = 120)] with one-third in Early Perimenopause [EP (n = 56)]" Section: Participants.

facts.claim: Endometriosis affects an estimated 6-11% of reproductive-aged women and is associated with high morbidity including infertility.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Endometriosis affects an estimated 6–11% of reproductive-aged women and is associated with a high degree of morbidity" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: In a combined model, women in the second tertile of chromium had increased odds of endometriosis diagnosis versus the lowest tertile (aOR=2.32; 95% CI: 1.42, 3.79).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women in the second tertile of chromium relative to women in the lowest tertile were at increased odds of diagnosis (aOR=2.32; 95% CI: 1.42, 3.79)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Urine specimens were analyzed for 20 trace elements via ICP-MS including antimony, arsenic, barium, beryllium, cadmium, cesium, chromium, cobalt, copper, lead, manganese, mercury, molybdenum, nickel, tellurium, thallium, tin, tungsten, uranium, and zinc.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Urine specimens were analyzed for 20 trace elements using a Perkin Elmer ELAN DRC II ICP-MS" Section: Urine Trace Elements.

facts.claim: Endometriosis in the operative cohort was diagnosed using the clinical gold standard of visualized disease at laparoscopy/laparotomy, with severity staged by Revised American Society for Reproductive Medicine classification.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "In the operative cohort, endometriosis was defined using the clinical gold standard of visualized disease" Section: Data Collection.

facts.claim: MRI sensitivity is higher for stages 3 and 4 endometriosis and limited for detecting stages 1-2 (mild/minimal) disease.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the limited sensitivity of the MRI for detecting stages 1–2 endometriosis, defined as mild/minimal disease" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Urinary cadmium reflects long-term exposure (kidney deposition over time) while blood cadmium reflects recent exposure on the order of days to weeks.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "urinary cadmium is a marker of long-term exposure, reflecting deposition in the kidney over time. Blood cadmium reflects recent exposure" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Of a panel of trace elements measured in blood and urine, only cadmium, chromium and copper were associated with endometriosis, and only in the operative cohort when categorized in tertiles.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "we observed only three trace elements (i.e., cadmium, chromium and copper) as being associated with endometriosis, but only in the operative cohort" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: The ENDO Study used a matched cohort design with 495 women in the operative cohort scheduled for laparoscopy/laparotomy and 131 women in a population cohort, recruited 2007-2009 in Utah and California.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "In total, 495 and 131 women comprised the operative and population cohorts, respectively." Section: Study Design and Population.

facts.claim: Indications for surgery in the operative cohort were: pelvic pain (44%), pelvic mass (16%), menstrual irregularities (13%), tubal ligation (10%), uterine leiomyomata (10%), and infertility (7%).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "pelvic pain (44%), pelvic mass (16%), menstrual irregularities (13%), tubal ligation (10%), uterine leiomyomata (10%), and infertility (7%)" Section: Study Design and Population.

facts.claim: Endometriosis incidence in the operative and population cohorts was 41% and 11%, respectively.

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the incidence of endometriosis in the two cohorts was 41% and 11%, respectively" Section: Results.

facts.claim: In the operative cohort, women with endometriosis had significantly lower mean blood cadmium concentrations (0.28 µg/L; 95% CI 0.25-0.31) than unaffected women (0.34; 95% CI 0.31-0.37).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women with endometriosis had significantly lower mean concentrations of blood cadmium than unaffected women (0.28; 95% CI: 0.25, 0.31 and 0.34" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Highest tertile of blood cadmium was associated with reduced odds of endometriosis diagnosis in the operative cohort (aOR=0.55; 95% CI: 0.31, 0.98).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "blood cadmium was associated with a reduced odds of diagnosis irrespective of statistical model (aOR=0.55; 95% CI: 0.31, 0.98)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Women in the second versus first tertile of urinary chromium had approximately twofold increased odds of endometriosis diagnosis (aOR=1.97; 95% CI: 1.21, 3.19).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women in the second versus first tertile of chromium had approximately a twofold increased odds of diagnosis (aOR=1.97; 95% CI: 1.21, 3.19)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Women in the third versus first tertile of urinary copper had greater than twofold increased odds of endometriosis diagnosis (aOR=2.66; 95% CI: 1.26, 5.64).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Women in the third versus first tertile of copper had greater than a twofold increased odds of diagnosis (aOR=2.66; 95% CI: 1.26, 5.64)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: In a combined model with cadmium, chromium and copper, highest tertile of cadmium remained associated with reduced odds of endometriosis (aOR=0.52; 95% CI: 0.29, 0.93).

source.title: Trace elements and endometriosis: the ENDO study
source.authors: Pollack AZ, Louis GMB, Chen Z, Peterson CM, Sundaram R, Croughan MS, Sun L, Hediger ML, Stanford JB, Varner MW, Palmer CD, Steuerwald AJ, Parsons PJ

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women in the highest tertile of cadmium had a reduced odds of endometriosis diagnosis (aOR=0.52; 95% CI: 0.29, 0.93)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Cervical mucus monitoring is performed via vulvar observations excluding days of menstrual flow, with no internal vaginal or cervical checking required; mucus is classified by appearance and sensation.

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "CMM...is performed via vulvar observations (excluding days of menstrual flow). Internal checking of the vagina or cervix is not required." Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: Among 331 TTC participants aged 30-44, 70% were under 35 years, 19% were 35-37, and 11% were 38 or older; 76% were Caucasian, 71% had a master's degree or PhD, and 57% had a previous pregnancy.

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "70% of women were <35 years of age, 19% were 35–37 years, and 11% were 38 years or older. Participants tended to be Caucasian (76%)" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Cycles in which women consistently performed cervical mucus monitoring had an adjusted fecundability ratio of 2.32 (95% CI 1.23-4.40) compared with non-monitored cycles, after adjusting for age, race, previous pregnancy, BMI, intercourse frequency, and urinary LH monitoring.

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "benefit of CMM remained after adjusting for intercourse frequency in the fertile window (FR = 2.32; 95% CI, 1.23–4.40)." Section: Results.

facts.claim: A statistically significant dose-response was observed: increasing consistency of cervical mucus monitoring was positively associated with increasing fecundability (P=.01).

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "A trend of increasing fecundability with increasing frequency of CMM was also noted (P=.01)." Section: Results.

facts.claim: Urinary LH monitoring alone produces false-positive results in approximately 7% of cycles in an infertile population and does not allow prospective determination of the entire fertile window.

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Urinary LH monitoring alone results in false-positive results in approximately 7% of cycles in an infertile population" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: The ClearBlue Easy Fertility Monitor (urinary LH plus estrone glucuronide) failed to identify the onset of the fertile window approximately 40% of the time when compared with self-assessed cervical mucus monitoring.

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the CBEFM failed to identify the onset of the fertile window approximately 40% of the time" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: In the TTC cohort, any cervical mucus monitoring was used by 42% of participants, more common than urinary LH monitoring (27%) or basal body temperature monitoring (30%).

source.title: Cervical mucus monitoring prevalence and associated fecundability in women trying to conceive
source.authors: Evans-Hoeker E, Pritchard DA, Long DL, Herring AH, Steiner AZ, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "any use of CMM (42% of participants) was more common than the use of urinary LH (27%) or basal body temperature monitoring (30%) in our study" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial enrolled 133 healthy early postmenopausal women in Vancouver, Canada from 2003-2009 testing oral micronized progesterone 300 mg daily versus placebo for 3 months.

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women
source.authors: Prior JC, Elliott TG, Norman E, Stajic V, Hitchcock CL

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "randomized, double-blind, 3-month placebo-controlled trial of progesterone (300 mg daily) among 133 healthy postmenopausal women in Vancouver" Section: Abstract/Methods.

facts.claim: Endothelium-independent vasodilator response to sodium nitroprusside was similar between progesterone and placebo arms, indicating progesterone does not alter smooth-muscle vascular reactivity.

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women
source.authors: Prior JC, Elliott TG, Norman E, Stajic V, Hitchcock CL

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Responses to the endothelium-independent vasodilator sodium nitroprusside (SNP) were similar on progesterone and placebo" Section: Endothelial Function.

facts.claim: Total-to-HDL-cholesterol ratio increased on progesterone versus placebo (difference 0.18, 95% CI 0.04 to 0.33, p=0.013), driven by the HDL-C decrease.

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women
source.authors: Prior JC, Elliott TG, Norman E, Stajic V, Hitchcock CL

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Total to HDL-C Ratio... 0.18 (0.04 to 0.33) 0.013" Section: Table 3.

facts.claim: Sample size for venous occlusion plethysmography was 25 participants per arm, providing 80% power to detect a 30% progesterone-versus-placebo difference in forearm blood flow.

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women
source.authors: Prior JC, Elliott TG, Norman E, Stajic V, Hitchcock CL

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Sample size for the venous occlusion plethysmography was 25 participants per arm, chosen for 80% power to detect a 30% difference" Section: Participants and Procedures.

facts.claim: D-dimer trended upward on progesterone (315.7 to 352.4 µg/L FEU) versus stable placebo (290.7 to 277.4), but difference was not statistically significant (95% CI -7.3 to 113.2, p=0.083).

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women
source.authors: Prior JC, Elliott TG, Norman E, Stajic V, Hitchcock CL

facts.claim: Eligibility required women to be 1-11 years since last menstruation, not on hormones for >6 months, non-smoking, without diabetes, hypertension, heart disease, or related medications.

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women
source.authors: Prior JC, Elliott TG, Norman E, Stajic V, Hitchcock CL

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women were 1–11 y since last menstruation, not using hormones (for .6 months), non-smoking, without diabetes, hypertension, heart disease" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: Endothelial function measured by venous occlusion plethysmography did not differ significantly between progesterone (487±189%) and placebo (408±278%), with 95% CI of difference -274 to 232, P=0.30.

source.title: Progesterone therapy, endothelial function and cardiovascular risk factors: a  3-month randomized, placebo-controlled trial in healthy early postmenopausal  women

source.authors: Harville EW, Mishra GD, Yeung E, Mumford SL, Schisterman EF, Jukic AM, Hatch EE, Mikkelsen EM, Jiang H, Ehrenthal DB, Porucznik CA, Wen SW, Harvey A, Symons Downs D, Yajnik C, Santillan D, Santillan M, McElrath TF, Woo JG, Urbina EM, Chavarro JE, Sotres-Alvarez D, Bazzano L, Zhang J, Steiner A, Gunderson EP, Wise LA, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "143 women attempting to conceive, prior pregnancy, greater Salt Lake City area, primarily white" Section: Table 3 (Time to Pregnancy in Couples of Proven Fecundity).

facts.claim: The HOPE Study (Home Observation of Periconceptional Exposures) enrolled 183 heterosexual couples trying to conceive in greater Salt Lake City, with 170 having follow-up information.

source.title: The Preconception Period analysis of Risks and Exposures Influencing health and Development (PrePARED) consortium
source.authors: Harville EW, Mishra GD, Yeung E, Mumford SL, Schisterman EF, Jukic AM, Hatch EE, Mikkelsen EM, Jiang H, Ehrenthal DB, Porucznik CA, Wen SW, Harvey A, Symons Downs D, Yajnik C, Santillan D, Santillan M, McElrath TF, Woo JG, Urbina EM, Chavarro JE, Sotres-Alvarez D, Bazzano L, Zhang J, Steiner A, Gunderson EP, Wise LA, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "183 couples, 170 with follow-up information" Section: Table 3 (HOPE Study).

facts.claim: The fertile window spans approximately 6 days, beginning about 5 days before ovulation and extending through the day of ovulation, with no documented conception on the day after ovulation.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Conception is possible from intercourse beginning about 5 days before ovulation extending through the day of ovulation." Section: The Fertile Window.

facts.claim: In a Billings Ovulation Method study, couples of normal fertility who had intercourse on the peak day had a 67% probability of pregnancy, and intercourse up to 3 days before peak day with favorable discharge yielded a 55% probability.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "couples of normal fertility who have intercourse on the peak day have a 67% probability of pregnancy" Section: Fertility Charting of Vaginal Discharge.

facts.claim: In a study of 100 couples conceiving without fertility awareness, half conceived by 3 months, 75% by 6 months, and over 90% by 12 months.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "In a study of 100 couples who conceived without fertility awareness, half had done so by 3 months, 75% by 6 months, and over 90% by 12 months." Section: Implications of Timed Intercourse for Infertility.

facts.claim: In a cohort study, 35% of untreated infertile couples (average length of infertility 3.1 years at entry) conceived spontaneously during follow-up of up to 7 years, with about 75% of pregnancies occurring in the first year.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "35% of untreated infertile couples (average length of infertility at entry 3.1 years) conceived in follow-up up to 7 years" Section: Implications of Timed Intercourse for Infertility.

facts.claim: Spermatozoa can survive for up to 6 days in properly estrogenized cervical mucus.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "spermatozoa can survive for up to 6 days in properly estrogenized cervical mucus." Section: The Fertile Window.

facts.claim: In women with normal fertility, only 30% had their entire fertile window completely within cycle days 10-17, and on each day between cycle days 6 and 21 at least 10% of women with regular cycles were within their fertile window.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "only 30% had their entire fertile window (as determined by urinary hormones) completely within days 10 and 17 of the menstrual cycle" Section: Calendar Calculations.

facts.claim: Approximately 3% of women with regular cycles were still within their fertile window on the day of expected menses by calendar calculation, making calendar methods unreliable for timing intercourse to conceive.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "on the day of expected menses (by calendar calculation), about 3% of women with regular cycles were still in their fertile window." Section: Calendar Calculations.

facts.claim: In a validation study of the ClearPlan Easy Fertility Monitor across 150 cycles, ovulation was detected by ultrasound within the 2 days of 'peak' fertility in 91% of cycles, with no cases of ovulation occurring before 'peak' fertility.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "ovulation was detected by ultrasound within the 2 days of \"peak\" fertility in 91% of cycles. In no cases did ovulation occur before \"peak\" fertility." Section: ClearPlan Easy Fertility Monitor.

facts.claim: The peak day identified by women using fertility charting correlates with the timing of ovulation within 2 or 3 days in all cases, as measured by serum hormones, urinary hormones, and follicular ultrasound.

source.title: Timing intercourse to achieve pregnancy: current evidence
source.authors: Stanford JB, White GL, Hatasaka H

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The peak day as identified by women correlates well with the timing of ovulation (2 or 3 days in all cases)" Section: Fertility Charting of Vaginal Discharge.

facts.claim: Women in this BC cohort showed no statistically significant difference in self-reported median age at menopause between women living with HIV (49.0 years, IQR 45.3-53.0) and HIV-negative women (50.0 years, IQR 46.0-53.0).

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "49.0 [45.3 to 53.0] vs. 50.0 [46.0 to 53.0] years; p = 0.28" Section: 3.3. Age at Menopause.

facts.claim: Prolonged amenorrhea is reported in approximately 20% of women living with HIV, much higher than the general population frequency.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "women have been reported to experience prolonged amenorrhea at a frequency of 20%, much higher than that of the general population" Section: 1. Introduction.

facts.claim: WHO defines menopause as the lack of a menstrual period for at least 12 consecutive months; age at menopause is calculated as one year after the final menstruation.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "The World Health Organization defines menopause as the lack of a menstrual period for at least 12 consecutive months" Section: 1. Introduction.

facts.claim: Median menopause age in both BC cohort groups was lower than the general Canadian population average of 51.0 years, suggesting shared socio-economic factors influence menopause timing.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "age at menopause was lower than the general Canadian population average of 51.0 years in both groups" Section: 4. Discussion.

facts.claim: When restricted to biochemically confirmed menopause (FSH-based), median menopause age was 50.0 years (IQR 46.0-53.0) for women living with HIV vs 51.0 years (IQR 46.0-53.0) for HIV-negative women, p = 0.54.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "50.0 [46.0 to 53.0] vs. 51.0 [46.0 to 53.0] years; p = 0.54" Section: 3.3. Age at Menopause.

facts.claim: Biochemically confirmed menopause was defined as amenorrhea ≥12 months plus either age ≥60 years or FSH levels ≥25 IU/mL.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "amenorrhea for at least 12 months, and either age ≥ 60 years or with FSH levels ≥ 25 IU/mL, were considered to have biochemically confirmed menopause" Section: 2.4.

facts.claim: Concordance between self-reported and biochemically confirmed menopause status was 92.5% in women living with HIV and 93.8% in HIV-negative women.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "concordance between self-report and biochemical confirmation of menopause in 92.5% of women living with HIV and 93.8% of HIV-negative women" Section: 3.2.

facts.claim: Among 26 women with discordance between self-reported and biochemically confirmed reproductive phase, 19 (73.1%) had current or past substance use.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Among those with discordance between self-report and biochemically confirmed reproductive phase, 19/26 (73.1%) had current or past substance use" Section: 3.2.

facts.claim: Current tobacco smoking was the only variable independently associated with lower age at menopause in univariate median regression (β = -3.00 years, 95% CI -4.95 to -1.05; p < 0.001).

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "current tobacco smoking was the only variable associated with lower age at menopause (β (95% CI): −3.00 (−4.95 to −1.05); p < 0.001)" Section: 3.3.

facts.claim: Rates of early menopause (<45 years) were similar between women living with HIV (16.9%, 14/83) and HIV-negative women (16.3%, 15/92), p = 1.00.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.claim: Primary ovarian insufficiency (menopause age <40) prevalence did not differ between groups: 9.64% (8/83) of women living with HIV vs 8.70% (8/92) of HIV-negative women, p = 0.83.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data
source.authors: Shayda A Swann, Elizabeth M King, Shelly Tognazzini, Amber R Campbell, Sofia L A Levy, Neora Pick, Jerilynn C Prior, Chelsea Elwood, Mona Loutfy, Valerie Nicholson, Angela Kaida, Hélène C F Côté, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "8/83 (9.64%) women living with HIV and 8/92 (8.70%) HIV-negative women had POI (p = 0.83)" Section: 3.3.

facts.claim: In this BC cohort, 16.5% of all women met criteria for early menopause and 9.1% for POI, far exceeding general population averages of 7.6% and 2% respectively.

source.title: Age at Natural Menopause in Women Living with HIV: A Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Self-Reported and Biochemical Data

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "16.5% of all women in our cohort met the criteria for early menopause and 9.1% for POI, much higher than the population averages of 7.6% and 2%" Section: 4. Discussion.

facts.claim: A single non-oncologic gynecologic surgeon diagnosed and curatively treated 4 rare cancers over 3 years using Near-Contact Laparoscopy (NCL) + Systematic Mapping of the Abdomen and Pelvis (S-MAP) during fertility/pain surgery.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'all cases of malignancies (n=4) by a single surgeon were included within a two year follow up period.' And: 'The single surgeon utilized the two standardized techniques of diagnostic laparoscopy to provide a thorough evaluation of reproductive disorders and, subsequent, incidental finding and curative treatment of four non-gynecologic cancers.' Context: Whittaker NM, AAGL 53rd Global Congress on MIGS, Nov 16-19, 2024, New Orleans.

facts.claim: All 4 cancers in the NCL/S-MAP case series were cured by surgery after subsequent imaging and workup; no chemotherapy or radiation was required in the documented cases.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'All of them were cured by surgery after further work up (including subsequent imaging and surgeries).' And for Case C: 'No chemotherapy or radiation was indicated.' Section: Interventions + Case C.

facts.claim: Well-differentiated papillary mesothelioma (WDPM) found during gynecologic fertility surgery is described only in case reports in the literature.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'The second malignancy (WDPM) is limited to case reports in the literature.' Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Endometriosis, infertility, and common shared exposures are not reported in the literature as risk factors for appendiceal neuroendocrine tumors or WDPM (ref: PMID 36176500).

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'Or, perhaps, endometriosis, infertility, or common exposures of these patient populations could be a risk factor for these cancers. However, these risk factors are not reported in the literature (PMID: 36176500).' Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Neuroendocrine tumors of the appendix have an incidence of 0.95 per 100,000.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'Three out of four of these cancers were neuroendocrine tumors of the appendix which have an incidence of 0.95 in 100,000.' Section: Discussion. Source: Whittaker NM, AAGL 2024 case series.

facts.claim: The statistical likelihood of a single surgeon incidentally finding 3 neuroendocrine tumors of the appendix is approximately 0.0000000001 (1 in 10 billion).

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'That means that the likelihood of 3 cases found by a single surgeon are 0.0000000001.' Section: Discussion. Inference: surgical technique (NCL/S-MAP) drives above-baseline detection of appendiceal cancers otherwise missed at laparoscopy.

facts.claim: Case A: NCL/S-MAP identified a 2 mm well-differentiated papillary mesothelioma (WDPM) on the right pelvic sidewall during fertility/pain surgery; further workup negative.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'In the NCL surgery using S-MAP, a 2 mm lesion of Well-differentiated papillary mesothelioma (WDPM) was found in the right pelvic sidewall. Further work up negative.' Section: Measurements and Main Results, Case A.

facts.claim: Case B: 27-year-old G0 presenting for second-opinion dysmenorrhea had NCL/S-MAP surgery yielding a 0.5 mm well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor with free margins and negative lymph nodes.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: '27 yo G0 presented for a second opinion for dysmenorrhea after surgery for endometriosis by faculty at an academic center. In the NCL/SPAM surgery, pathology came back positive for well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor, 0.5 mm, free margins, negative lymph nodes.' Section: Measurements and Main Results, Case B. Note: source typo 'NCL/SPAM' = NCL/S-MAP.

facts.claim: Case C: 33-year-old G3P2012 with irregular cycles, dysmenorrhea, and infertility had NCL/S-MAP surgery removing a stage IV well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of appendix (2.5 cm mass) plus 2 cancerous pelvic specimens (0.3-0.8 cm); subsequent hemicolectomy negative; in remission at 2 years.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'With NCL/S-MAP surgery, she underwent surgery which removed stage IV well differentiated Neuroendocrine tumor of appendix (mass 2.5 cm) plus 2 cancerous specimens from the pelvis (0.3 to 0.8 cm). With oncology, she underwent hemicolectomy. Lymph nodes, margins, and pelvis were negative. No chemotherapy or radiation was indicated. After two years, she is in remission.' Section: Measurements and Main Results, Case C.

facts.claim: Case D: 27-year-old G1P1 previously recommended for hysterectomy had NCL/S-MAP surgery revealing appendiceal/cecal mass, terminal ileum mass, bilateral endometriomas, and frozen pelvis; pathology returned grade 1 neuroendocrine tumor of appendix (1.5 cm); she had FSH 34.6 and achieved spontaneous pregnancy after additional excision surgeries, Goretex adhesion prevention, and DHEA supplementation.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'Appendectomy with partial cecectomy was performed and pathology resulted as grade 1 neuroendocrine tumor of appendix (1.5 cm)... she was found to have an FSH of 34.6 and achieved spontaneous pregnancy after additional excision surgeries, adhesion prevention (including with Goretex membrane) and DHEA supplementation.' Section: Measurements and Main Results, Case D.

facts.claim: Near-Contact Laparoscopy (NCL) was originally coined by Dr. David Redwine (PMID: 3190209).

source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'The Near-Contact Laparoscopy (NCL) technique was originally coined by Dr. Redwine (PMID: 3190209).' Section: Interventions.

facts.claim: The Systematic Mapping of the Abdomen and Pelvis (S-MAP) method was described in the NaProTechnology textbook by Dr. Thomas Hilgers; the term 'S-MAP' was coined by Dr. Naomi Whittaker.

source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.claim: In the CARMA cohort, women with HIV had lower median AMH levels (1.5 ng/ml) compared to HIV-negative controls (2.3 ng/ml) across reproductive ages 12-50.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "WWH had shorter relative LTL (7.2 vs. 7.5) and lower AMH (1.5 vs. 2.3 ng/ml) than controls." Section: Participant characteristics.

facts.claim: At initial visit among WWH in CARMA, 76% were on antiretroviral therapy, 42% had detectable HIV viral load, and 46% had a peak viral load >100,000 copies/ml.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "at the initial visit, 76% were on ART, 42% had a detectable HIV pVL, and 46% had a peak pVL > 100 000 copies/ml" Section: Participant characteristics.

facts.claim: Among women under 35, HIV status was independently associated with 20% lower AMH levels after multivariable adjustment, while each year of age increase corresponded to a 26% AMH increase.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Each year of increase in age was associated with a 26% AMH increase, although WWH had 20% lower AMH levels overall." Section: Women under 35 years old.

facts.claim: Among women aged 35 or older, each unit of relative leukocyte telomere length was associated with 43% higher odds of having AMH >=2 ng/ml, while each year of age was associated with 23% lower odds.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "each year was associated with 23% lower odds of AMH ≥2 ng/ml and every unit of relative LTL with 43% higher odds of AMH ≥2 ng/ml." Section: Women aged 35 years or older.

facts.claim: Current opioid use in women aged 35 or older was independently associated with a 77% decrease in odds of having AMH >=2 ng/ml, after multivariable adjustment.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Currently using opioids was independently associated with a 77% decrease in the odds of AMH ≥2 ng/ml." Section: Women aged 35 years or older.

facts.claim: AMH levels in the general reproductive lifespan rise into the mid-twenties then plateau and decline beginning in the early to mid-thirties; the threshold AMH >=2 ng/ml is commonly used in fertility literature to assess odds of live birth.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "AMH initially increases into the mid-twenties, then plateaus and begins to decline in the early to mid-thirties" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: In the CARMA cohort cross-sectional analysis, age alone explained 49% of variance in AMH levels (R2 = 0.49) via curvilinear regression across all 462 participants aged 12-50.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "A univariate curvilinear regression demonstrated a large proportion of variance in AMH was explained by age alone (R2 = 0.49)" Section: Anti-Müllerian hormone and age.

facts.claim: AMH was quantified using the picoAMH ELISA (Ansh Labs AL-124) with linear range 25-6000 pg/ml; whole blood relative leukocyte telomere length was measured by multiplex quantitative PCR.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "AMH levels were quantified using the picoAMH ELISA kit (product ID: AL-124, Ansh Labs, Webster, Texas, USA)" Section: Leukocyte telomere length and anti-Müllerian hormone.

facts.claim: In an external study of 2621 WWH and 941 controls, lower CD4+ cell count was associated with lower AMH levels even among controls with healthy CD4+ counts; CARMA found no such association in its younger sample.

source.title: Lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels are associated with HIV in reproductive age women and shorter leukocyte telomere length among late reproductive age women
source.short_citation: Clara E Van Ommen et al., 2023
source.authors: Clara E Van Ommen, Anthony Y Y Hsieh, Arianne Y Albert, Elana R Kimmel, Helene C F Cote, Evelyn J Maan, Jerilynn C Prior, Neora Pick, Melanie C M Murray

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "A study of 2621 WWH and 941 controls found an association between lower CD4+ cell count and lower AMH levels" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: In the PRESTO prospective cohort, use of any menstrual cycle tracking app at baseline was associated with a 20% increase in per-cycle fecundability (FR 1.20, 95% CI 1.13-1.28 for selected apps; FR 1.21, 95% CI 1.13-1.30 for other apps).

facts.claim: In women with normal ovarian function, oral contraceptives caused a significant decline in serum AMH within 3 months of use (WMD -1.43 ng/mL, 95% CI -2.05 to -0.80, P<0.00001).

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: Meta-analysis of 8 self-control studies, n=579 for overall OC analysis; subgroup <=3 months n=165. Quote: 'Serum AMH level decreased significantly (WMD: -1.43, 95%CI: -2.05 to -0.80; P < 0.00001) in the subgroup of use time <= 3 months (n = 165).'

facts.claim: With oral contraceptive use for more than 3 months, there was no significant effect on serum AMH levels (WMD -0.09, 95% CI -0.37 to 0.19, P=0.45), suggesting granulosa cell adaptation to FSH downregulation.

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'With use for more than 3 months or even longer, there was no significant effect on serum AMH levels (WMD: -0.09, 95%CI: -0.37 to 0.19; P = 0.45).'

facts.claim: Metformin pretreatment in PCOS patients caused a significant decrease in serum AMH (WMD -1.79 ng/mL, 95% CI -2.32 to -1.26, P<0.00001), affecting both obese (WMD -1.34) and non-obese (WMD -1.87) patients.

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: Meta-analysis of 12 studies, n=362. Quote: 'MET (2-12 months) led to a significant decrease in serum AMH in PCOS patients (WMD: -1.79, 95%CI: -2.32 to -1.26, P < 0.00001).'

facts.claim: GnRH agonist pretreatment in endometriosis patients caused a transient rise in serum AMH at 1 month (WMD 0.87, 95% CI 0.00 to 1.73, P=0.05) followed by a decrease at 3 months (WMD -0.26, 95% CI -0.48 to -0.04, P=0.02).

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: 10 sets of data from 5 studies, n=1099. Quote: 'After 1 month, there was a transient increase (WMD: 0.87; 95%CI: 0.00 to 1.73; P = 0.05), and the serum AMH decreased after 3 months (WMD: -0.26; 95%CI: -0.48 to -0.04; P = 0.02).'

facts.claim: DHEA supplementation (75 mg, 3 times daily) in women with diminished ovarian reserve or poor ovarian response caused a significant increase in serum AMH (WMD 0.18 ng/mL, 95% CI 0.09 to 0.27, P<0.0001).

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: Meta-analysis of 8 studies, n=431. Quote: 'DHEA led to a significant increase in serum AMH (WMD: 0.18, 95% CI: 0.09 to 0.27; P < 0.0001).'

facts.claim: Vitamin D supplementation (2000-5000 IU/week for 2 weeks to 6 months) increased serum AMH in non-PCOS patients (WMD 0.77, 95% CI 0.33 to 1.21, P=0.0007) but not significantly in PCOS patients.

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: Meta-analysis of 9 sets of data, n=316. Quote: 'In non-PCOS patients VD supplementation caused a statistically significant increase in serum AMH (WMD: 0.77, 95%CI: 0.33 to 1.21; P = 0.0007).'

facts.claim: Clomiphene citrate (50 mg/day for 1-3 cycles) caused a significant decrease in serum AMH in PCOS patients (WMD -0.89, 95% CI -1.55 to -0.23, P=0.008), more pronounced in non-obese patients (WMD -1.24, 95% CI -1.87 to -0.61, P=0.0001).

source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: Meta-analysis of 8 studies, n=869; non-obese subgroup n=376. Quotes: 'CC pretreatment (1-3 cycles) can cause a significant decrease in serum AMH levels in PCOS patients (WMD: -0.89, 95%CI: -1.55 to -0.23; P = 0.008).' 'in non-obese (BMI < 25 kg/m2) patients CC pretreatment (n = 376, 2 sets of data) caused a significant reduction in serum AMH levels (WMD: -1.24, 95%CI: -1.87 to -0.61; P = 0.0001).'

facts.claim: Letrozole (2.5 mg/day for 5 days over 3-6 cycles) had no significant effect on serum AMH levels in the short term (WMD -0.09, 95% CI -0.22 to 0.04, P=0.16).

source.title: The effect of medication on serum anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels in women of  reproductive age: a meta-analysis
source.authors: Yin WW, Huang CC, Chen YR, Yu DQ, Jin M, Feng C

facts.verification_notes: FEM analysis of 3 studies, n=397. Quote: 'LET (3-6 cycles) have no significant effect on AMH levels in the short term (WMD: -0.09, 95%CI: -0.22 to 0.04; P = 0.16).'

facts.claim: Because serum AMH is influenced by multiple common medications, it is recommended that AMH testing be performed at least 3 months after stopping hormonal contraceptives or GnRH agonists to determine true ovarian reserve.

source.authors: Fehring RJ, Hanson L, Stanford JB

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "1) basal body temperature (BBT), 2) ovulation method, 3) LAM, 4) symptothermal, and 5) calendar/rhythm." Section: Natural Family Planning.

facts.claim: 23.1% of CNM respondents felt LAM was not reliable and 8.4% were unfamiliar with the method; only 34.8% indicated efficacy extends 6 months postpartum and 2.4% to 12 months.

facts.claim: PRESTO preconception cohort analysis of current menstrual cycle characteristics and fecundability included 1,260 regularly-cycling women contributing 5,719 cycles and 777 pregnancies.

source.authors: Wesselink AK, Wise LA, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Mikkelsen EM, Stanford JB, McKinnon CJ, Mahalingaiah S

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "includes 1,260 regularly-cycling women contributing 5,719 cycles and 777 pregnancies." Section: Results.

facts.claim: In the PRESTO cohort, 91% of women had average bleed length of 3–6 days and 3% reported heavy bleeds.

source.title: Menstrual cycle characteristics and fecundability in a North American preconception cohort
source.authors: Wesselink AK, Wise LA, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Mikkelsen EM, Stanford JB, McKinnon CJ, Mahalingaiah S

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Ninety-one percent of women had an average bleed length of 3–6 days, and 3% of women reported heavy bleeds." Section: Results.

facts.claim: In a Danish preconception cohort of 2,653 pregnancy planners, cycles <25 days were associated with reduced fecundability (FR=0.64) vs 27–29 day cycles.

source.title: Menstrual cycle characteristics and fecundability in a North American preconception cohort
source.authors: Wesselink AK, Wise LA, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Mikkelsen EM, Stanford JB, McKinnon CJ, Mahalingaiah S

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "cycles of <25 days were associated with reduced fecundability (FR=0.64) compared with cycle lengths of 27–29 days" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: In 6,271 IVF cycles in Sweden, cumulative pregnancy rates were lowest (16.9%) for cycles <26 days and highest (31.3%) for cycles 32–34 days.

source.title: Menstrual cycle characteristics and fecundability in a North American preconception cohort
source.authors: Wesselink AK, Wise LA, Hatch EE, Rothman KJ, Mikkelsen EM, Stanford JB, McKinnon CJ, Mahalingaiah S

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "cumulative pregnancy rates were lowest (16.9%) in women with cycles of <26 days and highest (31.3%) in women with cycles of 32–34 days" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: In a Spanish IVF donor cohort, recipients of donor cycles 25–26 days had RR=0.74 and donors with 34–35 day cycles had RR=1.37 for pregnancy vs 27–29 day cycles.

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "donors had cycle lengths of 25–26 and 34–35 days had the lowest (RR=0.74) and highest (RR=1.37) risk of pregnancy" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: PRESTO analysis of early life menstrual characteristics included 2,189 women contributing 9,832 cycles and 1,355 pregnancies; 51.4% had cycles regularize in <2 years and 21.8% never regularized.

facts.claim: In a prospective cohort of 111 women ages 18-35 with proven fertility followed for up to 7 cycles, 90 (81.1%) conceived and 76 (68.5%) achieved a live birth over an average follow-up of 4.01 cycles (SD 1.79).

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive
source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a total of 90 (81.1%) conceived and 76 (68.5%) achieved a live birth during the average follow-up time of 4.01 cycles" Section: Results.

facts.claim: Daily diaries captured perceived stress on a 0-10 scale alongside bleeding, intercourse, alcohol, tobacco, caffeinated coffee/tea/soda intake, medications, and illness, recorded daily until pregnancy or for 7 cycles.

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive
source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "perceived stress (scale with 0 being none and 10 being maximum)" Section: Perceived stress and covariate assessment.

facts.claim: Stress tertile cut points used in analysis were low (0.1-2.7), moderate (>2.7-4.1), and high (>4.1-7.2) on the daily 0-10 perceived stress scale.

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive
source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "high [ > 4.1–7.2], moderate [ > 2.7–4.1], and low [0.1–2.7]" Section: Abstract.

facts.claim: Cycle-specific intent to conceive substantially altered effect estimates and is identified by the authors as a critical confounder when modeling fecundability, since intent varies cycle to cycle.

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive
source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "we adjusted for cycle intent to conceive, which had a sub-stantial impact on the results of the model" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Daily urine LH was measured from cycle day 2 through end of cycle or day 31 using a blinded ClearBlue Fertility Monitor; peak LH day was identified by two trained reviewers with a third adjudicating discrepancies.

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive
source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "daily urine testing from cycle day 2 up through the end of the cycle or day 31 with a blinded version of the ClearBlue® Fertility Monitor" Section: Perceived stress and covariate assessment.

facts.claim: Among women with proven fertility, high versus low daily perceived stress showed no adverse effect on fecundability (adjusted FOR 1.11, 95% CI 0.58-2.14); moderate versus low stress also showed no adverse effect (aFOR 1.37, 95% CI 0.71-2.67).

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive

facts.claim: Pregnancy was defined as a positive urine hCG test sensitive to 25 mIU/mL or higher (Clearblue pregnancy tests) following missed menstruation or clinical symptoms.

source.title: Daily perceived stress and time to pregnancy: A prospective cohort study of women trying to conceive
source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a positive result in urine human chorionic gonadotropin testing at a study visit, sensitive to a concentration of 25 mIU/mL or higher" Section: Pregnancy assessment.

facts.claim: Fertile windows were defined relative to the peak LH surge day: broad fertile window = -6 to +3 days (10 days total); narrow fertile window = -3 to +1 days (5 days total).

source.authors: Park J, Porucznik CA, Christensen K, Stanford JB, Schliep KC

facts.claim: Mean intercourse frequency over the study period was 3.3 (SD 1.3) acts during the broad 10-day fertile window and 2.1 (SD 0.8) acts during the narrow 5-day fertile window.

facts.claim: Dr. David Redwine performed near-contact laparoscopy for endometriosis removal on nearly 400 women since 1979.

source.title: Endometriosis. Obtaining relief via 'near-contact' laparoscopy
source.authors: Petersen NF, Rhoe J

facts.verification_notes: Quote: 'Since 1979, Dr Redwine has performed the near-contact laparoscopy for the removal of endometriosis on nearly 400 women.' Source: Petersen NF, Rhoe J. Endometriosis. Obtaining relief via 'near-contact' laparoscopy. AORN J. 1988 Oct;48(4):700-7. PMID 3190209. AORN J article reports on Redwine's clinical series; NCL technique attributed to Redwine beginning 1979.

facts.claim: 75% of Dr. Redwine's near-contact laparoscopy patients experienced complete relief of endometriosis symptoms; 20% experienced improvement from disabling to minimal pain.

facts.claim: Subfertility affects at least 15% of couples during their reproductive lives, but fewer than 30% of subfertile couples ultimately fail to achieve a desired live birth.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Subfertility is a common condition, affecting at least 15% of couples during their reproductive lives" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: A randomized controlled trial found that for unexplained subfertility, neither clomiphene nor intrauterine insemination was more effective than expectant management over 6 months.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "neither clomiphene nor intrauterine insemination were more effective than expectant management over 6 months" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: The truly sterile fraction among all couples varies by age, from 1% at 25 years to 5% at 35 years; the model assumed 5% of couples not conceiving after 2 years had true sterility.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "Recent estimates of truly sterile fraction among all couples vary by age, from 1% at 25 years to 5% at 35 years" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Evidence suggests the effective fertile window is narrower in many subfertile couples, perhaps only a couple of days, and targeting intercourse to the fertile window significantly improves pregnancy rates.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "the effective fertile window is narrower in many subfertile couples, perhaps only a couple of days" Section: Discussion.

facts.claim: Conservative treatments or simply consistent well-timed intercourse result in cumulative pregnancies equivalent to early IVF over a 1-3 year follow-up period in couples without definitive diagnosis.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "conservative treatments, or simply consistent well-timed intercourse, will result in cumulative pregnancies equivalent to early IVF" Section: Conclusions.

facts.claim: Only about half of subfertile couples seek any medical consultation, with substantial numbers of spontaneous conceptions occurring without treatment.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "only one-half of subfertile couples seek any medical consultation, because there are substantial numbers of spontaneous conceptions" Section: Introduction.

facts.claim: In the simulation baseline model, cumulative probability of clinically recognized pregnancy is 20%-25% in the first cycle and 85%-95% after 13 cycles in couples with regular intercourse.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "cumulative probability of clinically recognized pregnancy for the entire sample is 20%–25% in the first cycle and 85%–95% after 13 cycles" Section: Materials and Methods.

facts.claim: Reducing intercourse frequency from twice per week to once per week produces approximately a 40% reduction in probability of pregnancy per cycle.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "a reduction in frequency of intercourse resulting in a 40% reduction in probability of pregnancy per cycle" Section: Materials and Methods, treatment scenarios.

facts.claim: A Cochrane review of clomiphene versus placebo for unexplained subfertility found a 2.4-fold increase in pregnancy chances in the treatment group.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "comparing ovulation induction with clomiphene to placebo and found a 2.4-fold increase in pregnancy chances in the treatment group" Section: Materials and Methods.

facts.claim: In the Lintsen et al. Netherlands cohort, IVF couples completed an average of 1.8 cycles with cumulative pregnancy probability of 45% at 1 year, with 30% dropout after the first and 43% after the second unsuccessful cycle.

source.title: Cumulative pregnancy probabilities among couples with subfertility: effects of varying treatments
source.authors: Lynch CD, Simonsen SE, Stanford JB, Mikolajczyk RT

facts.verification_notes: Quote: "couples completed an average of 1.8 cycles of IVF, and the cumulate pregnancy probability of pregnancy at 1 year was 45%" Section: Materials and Methods.

facts.claim: IVF applied after 20 cycles of conservative treatment yields the highest cumulative pregnancy proportion at 26 cycles (about 2 years), with a much smaller proportion of the population requiring IVF.

facts.claim: In the Cerner COVID-19 cohort of 22,493 female patients aged 18-44 with COVID-19, 7.2% (n=1,609) were pregnant.

source.authors: Qeadan F, Mensah NA, Tingey B, Stanford JB

facts.claim: Pregnant COVID-19 patients had higher reported obesity prevalence than non-pregnant peers (20.6% vs. 17.1%, P<0.001) and higher gestational diabetes history (10.1% vs. 2.0%, P<0.001).

source.title: The risk of clinical complications and death among pregnant women with COVID-19 in the Cerner COVID-19 cohort: a retrospective analysis
