facts.claim: 4 rare cancers diagnosed and cured during fertility surgery using NCL/S-MAP
facts.body: Dr. Naomi Whittaker presented a case series at the AAGL 53rd Global Congress on MIGS (New Orleans, November 16-19, 2024) demonstrating that NCL/S-MAP techniques led to the incidental diagnosis and curative treatment of 4 rare cancers by a single non-oncologic gynecologic surgeon over 3 years. Three were neuroendocrine tumors of the appendix (incidence 0.95 per 100,000; probability of 3 by one surgeon: 0.0000000001). The fourth was a 2 mm well-differentiated papillary mesothelioma (WDPM). All 4 cancers were cured. 3 of 3 infertile patients subsequently achieved pregnancy. Case D notably used robotic-assisted laser excision of endometriosis. No standard criteria currently exist for how to perform diagnostic laparoscopy in gynecology.

source.title: Case Series: Diagnosis and Treatment of Four Rare Cancers by a Single Surgeon Due to Systematic Mapping and Near Contact Techniques
source.authors: Whittaker NM

facts.verification_notes: Linked via WS1 citation resolution (2026-04-06)

facts.claim: Systematic Mapping of the Abdomen and Pelvis (S-MAP)
facts.body: S-MAP (also written SMAP; Systematic Mapping of the Abdomen and Pelvis) is a standardized, reproducible protocol for comprehensive examination of all abdominal and pelvic structures during diagnostic laparoscopy. The method follows the same sequence every time: upper abdomen (diaphragm, gallbladder), lower abdomen (cecum, appendix, terminal ileum), and all pelvic structures (anterior and posterior cul-de-sac, uterosacral ligaments, broad ligaments, pelvic sidewalls, ovaries, fallopian tubes). The systematic evaluation sequence was described by Dr. Thomas Hilgers in the NaProTECHNOLOGY textbook (Chapter 63). The term 'S-MAP' was coined by Dr. Naomi Whittaker. S-MAP is used in combination with Near-Contact Laparoscopy (NCL), a technique originally described by Dr. David Redwine (PMID: 3190209).

source.raw_source_id: Hilgers TW, NaProTECHNOLOGY textbook Ch. 63, 2004; Whittaker NM, AAGL 2024 case series

facts.body: The main paper states 75% (205 of 273) of conceptions leading to live birth occurred within 12 months, and 93% (255 of 273) within 18 months. In the 2009 response to correspondence, Stanford et al. state '78% [of couples who conceived] within the first 12 months' -- this higher figure likely includes conceptions that did not lead to live birth. Use 75% (205/273) when citing live-birth conceptions specifically. Similar to Tham 2012's finding that 85% of live-birth conceptions occurred within 12 months. Both support treating for up to 24 months while noting most success happens in the first year.

facts.body: Clomiphene was the predominant ovulation stimulation medication, used by 75% of couples in the Stanford 2008 Irish practice. This is notably higher than Tham 2012 (37%) or iNEST (38%). The high clomiphene usage rate partly explains the 4.6% twin rate and reflects the treatment norms of the 1998-2002 era. This was the largest single-practice RRM outcome study until iNEST. The crude-to-adjusted gap (25.5% vs 52.8%) is large because of the high dropout rate, a limitation the authors acknowledge.

facts.claim: Among live births, there were 4.6% twin births
facts.body: Among live births in the Stanford 2008 cohort, there were 4.6% twin births. The abstract states this was a notable safety outcome compared to much higher multiple rates in ART. Compare to Tham 2012 which had 0% multiple gestations (clomiphene 37%, lower usage).

facts.claim: 21,803 women (weighted 1,022,597) in FL/MD/UT, 2004-2008, dual referent group design
facts.body: Population-based cross-sectional study using PRAMS (Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System) data from FL, MD, UT (2004-2008). N=21,803 women, weighted to represent 1,022,597 women with live births. 48.1% were trying to conceive; of those, 9.3% used fertility treatments. Treatment breakdown: OS 30.4%, ART 22.0%, other 19.9%, IUI 13.1%, treatment but none during conception month 14.6%. Adjusted for age, education, FPL, BMI, parity, race, ethnicity, insurance, marital status, pre-conception smoking. Dual referent group design: (1) all never-treated women, (2) subfertile women conceiving without treatment.

source.raw_source_id: Stanford JB, Simonsen SE, Baksh L. BJOG 2016;123:718-729 (PMID 26148540)

facts.claim: vs subfertile controls, all births: ART preterm OR 3.21 (95% CI 1.71, 6.07); singletons: no treatment significantly different from subfertile controls
facts.body: When referent group is subfertile women who conceived without treatment (all births): ART preterm <37wk OR 3.21 (95% CI 1.71, 6.07), very preterm OR 3.35 (95% CI 1.69, 6.65), LBW OR 3.20 (95% CI 1.93, 5.32). IUI and OS were NOT significant vs subfertile controls. This is the study's key finding: after controlling for subfertility, only ART (primarily through multiple gestation) retains significant risk. For singletons, NONE of the treatments were significantly different from subfertile controls.

source.raw_source_id: Stanford JB, Simonsen SE, Baksh L. BJOG 2016;123:718-729 (PMID 26148540)

facts.verification_notes: Promoted from local rrm-cli facts.json (170 curated facts)

facts.claim: Subfertility itself: VLBW OR 1.90 (95% CI 1.25, 2.89) vs never-treated women
facts.body: Subfertile women who received treatment at some point but no specific treatment during month of conception (a proxy for spontaneous conception in subfertile women) had OR 1.90 (95% CI 1.25, 2.89) for very low birthweight <1500g vs never-treated women. This was the only significant finding for the subfertile-no-treatment group. It demonstrates subfertility itself -- independent of treatment -- carries perinatal risk, supporting the RRM argument that addressing underlying pathology (the RRM approach) may be more important than bypassing it (the ART approach).

source.raw_source_id: Stanford JB, Simonsen SE, Baksh L. BJOG 2016;123:718-729 (PMID 26148540)

facts.verification_notes: Promoted from local rrm-cli facts.json (170 curated facts)

facts.claim: 54% (22/41) born at 37+ weeks gestation; 0% multiple gestations; 78% birth weight ≥2500g. Published and uncorrected in erratum.
facts.body: Among 41 live births (all singletons). Gestational age: 54% (22/41) born at 37+ weeks, 32% (13/41) at 32-37 weeks, 15% (6/41) at <32 weeks. Birth weight: 78% (32/41) at 2500g or greater, 10% (4/41) at <1500g. ANOMALY: The 46% preterm rate is an extreme outlier vs all other RRM studies (Stanford 2008: 86% term, Boyle 2018: 92% term, Boyle 2025: 96% term). The corrected PDF (July 2012 erratum) does NOT change Table 5 -- the 54% figure stands in the published corrected version. Small N=41, single Canadian family practice. The paper itself claims "low rates of preterm births" comparable to Stanford 2008, which is contradicted by its own data. Possible explanations: gestational age recording method in family practice, high-risk cohort (18% RPL, mean age 35.4), or data quality issues. Do not use for birth safety comparisons.

facts.claim: Unexplained infertility reduced from 40% to 1%, unexplained miscarriage from 29% to 2% after NPT evaluation
facts.body: Before NPT evaluation, 40% of women had unexplained infertility and 29% had unexplained miscarriage. After NPT evaluation: only 1% unexplained infertility and 2% unexplained miscarriage. NPT evaluation found: 62% low progesterone, 50% low luteal estrogen, 50% low follicular estrogen, 14% anovulation (vs 2% before), 6% PCOS (vs 3% before), 9% limited cervical mucus. This parallels Boyle 2025's finding (24% to 1%) in a different population, independently replicating the diagnostic yield of RRM evaluation.

facts.verification_notes: Promoted from local rrm-cli facts.json (170 curated facts)

facts.claim: Tham crude 38% / adjusted 66% vs Stanford crude 25.5% / adjusted 52.8%; difference attributed to cohort prognosis
facts.body: Tham 2012 had higher crude LBR (38% vs 25.5%) and adjusted LBR (66% vs 52.8%) than Stanford 2008. Tham's authors explicitly attribute this to the better initial prognosis of their cohort: shorter time trying (3.2 vs 5.6 years), less prior ART (8% vs 33%), similar age (35.4 vs 35.8). The comparison demonstrates that population characteristics, not practice quality, drive the crude rate differences between RRM studies. Both used life-table (Kaplan-Meier) methodology for adjusted rates.

facts.claim: 45-82% success for tubal anastomosis; 'significantly higher cumulative pregnancy rate than IVF' (Spirov)
facts.body: Even the Spirov REI textbook acknowledges tubal anastomosis (sterilization reversal) has a 'significantly higher cumulative pregnancy rate than IVF' and is 'more cost efficient.' Success rate: 45-82% for microsurgical sterilization reversal. Especially advantageous in women over 40 where IVF success is low. However, Spirov frames surgery as appropriate only 'in select circumstances' -- for 'ethical or religious objections, or financial restrictions' -- rather than as the first-line option the data supports. IVF per-cycle LBR at 43-44 is only 2.3% (SART), making tubal surgery substantially more effective for this age group.

facts.claim: 60.60% of PCOS patients experienced improvements in menstrual cycles after delivery
facts.body: Among 186 PCOS patients who delivered after ART, 132 had irregular cycles pre-pregnancy. Of these, 80 (60.60%) experienced improved menstrual cycles after delivery -- 67 fully recovered normal patterns, 13 partially improved. No significant differences between improved and non-improved groups in age, BMI, baseline hormones, basal antral follicles, or ART parameters. Improvement was not associated with any specific ART procedure. Mouse model showed elevated progesterone during pregnancy depletes large antral follicles via GATA2-mediated FSHR downregulation, enhancing post-withdrawal estrogen biosynthesis.

source.raw_source_id: Yang Q et al. Life Metabolism. 2026. doi:10.1093/lifemeta/oag004

facts.claim: Repeat surgery rate after optimal excision: 2.5% (15/620) vs historical ablation rate 40-60%
facts.body: In a prospective cohort of 620 women with histologically confirmed endometriosis (2012-2019), only 15 of 620 (2.5%) who received optimal excision surgery required repeat surgery. Historical rates of repeat surgery after ablation are 40-60% within 1-2 years. Endometriosis was found in 12 of 14 repeat surgeries (85.7%). No significant association between pre-operative hormonal suppression use and endometriosis stage progression. 59.4% had used hormonal suppression before excision surgery (median 36 months). Single surgeon (Patrick Yeung), single center (SLU Center of Endometriosis). Published in Acta Scientific Women's Health 7(1), January 2025.

source.raw_source_id: Yeung PP Jr, Mohan A, Gavard JA. Preprints.org 2024. doi:10.20944/preprints202409.1485.v1

facts.verification_notes: Promoted from local rrm-cli facts.json (170 curated facts)

facts.claim: No significant association between hormonal suppression use and stage of endometriosis
facts.body: Among 387 women with known stage and known hormonal suppression status before excision surgery: 62.2% of Stage I/II and 53.7% of Stage III/IV had used hormonal suppression (p=0.05). No significant overall association between any hormonal suppression variable and endometriosis stage when all four stages examined simultaneously. This provides implicit evidence that hormonal suppression does not prevent progression of endometriosis.
